title: The Sayings of St. Bernard and The Vision of St. Paul
summary: CME00150 is the repository copy of a University of Michigan encoding of Carl Horstmann's complete six-page 1874 article, which prints two distinct anonymous Middle English poems from Bodleian Library MS. Laud Misc. 108: The Sayings of St. Bernard on ff. 198ra-199rb and The Vision of St. Paul on ff. 199rb-200vb. The two transmission paths share the edition, manuscript, and one boundary canvas but remain separately scoped. DIMEV 5215 identifies five Sayings witnesses: the Laud copy, Bodleian MSS Add. E. 6 (R) and Digby 86, British Library Harley MS 2253, and the Auchinleck Manuscript fragment. Complete public facsimiles of Laud, Digby, and the provider-published Auchinleck project are cached. Add. E. 6 has a catalog and mediated whole-roll reproduction route, plus Joseph B. Monda's 1970 scholarly edition in a cached complete journal-volume scan. Harley has the current catalog/request route, the complete METS scholarly edition, and Neil R. Ker's purchasable 1965 partial monochrome facsimile covering the target, but no verified public downloadable copy of Ker's volume. Abstract Middle English work nodes preserve the distinction between each witness and the underlying work, and qualified version-of paths connect the poems to the Pseudo-Bernardian Meditationes tradition and Latin Visio Pauli Redaction IV without inventing physical Latin exemplars. Brandes's 1885 printing of Redaction IV is a supporting edition, not the Vision translator's exemplar. Exact printed-page, folio, PDF-page, and IIIF-image mappings are recorded. Historical texts and editions are assessed separately from modern catalog data, scholarly editions, and manuscript photographs.
review_notes: Middle English is the language of both medieval poems. German occurs only in Horstmann's headings, byline, and editorial notes and is not transferred to the abstract works.
The Sayings and the Vision are independent work identities. They share Laud fol. 199r / IIIF canvas 413 because the first ends and the second begins on that manuscript surface.
Internet Archive's item metadata gives 1846 as a serial-level date, but the scanned volume title page and article supply 1874; 1874 is used for this volume and article.
Bernard of Clairvaux is a traditional association inherited through the poem and catalogs, not established authorship of the Middle English adaptation.

entities.label: Carl Horstmann, Die Sprüche des h. Bernhard und die Vision des h. Paulus nach Ms. Laud 108 (1874)

date_statements.label: publication
date_statements.notes: The scanned volume title page controls over Internet Archive's inherited 1846 serial-start date.

bibliographic.citation: Carl Horstmann, ‘Die Sprüche des h. Bernhard und die Vision des h. Paulus nach Ms. Laud 108,’ Archiv für das Studium der neueren Sprachen und Literaturen, 29th year, vol. 52 (Braunschweig: George Westermann, 1874), 33-38.
bibliographic.title: Die Sprüche des h. Bernhard und die Vision des h. Paulus nach Ms. Laud 108
bibliographic.container_title: Archiv für das Studium der neueren Sprachen und Literaturen

physical_description.format: journal article and diplomatic manuscript edition

notes: Part I, The Sayings of St. Bernard, occupies pp. 33-35; Part II, The Vision of St. Paul, begins on p. 35 and continues through p. 38.
Horstmann prints folio milestones and manuscript-reading corrections that establish direct use of Laud Misc. 108 rather than an intervening edition.

entities.label: Internet Archive complete scan of Archiv volume 52
entities.reproduction_format: 496-page PDF, OCR text, and browser page images

entities.label: Bodleian Library MS. Laud Misc. 108
alternate_labels: Oxford, Bodleian Library, MS Laud Misc. 108

date_statements.label: manuscript production
date_statements.value: late thirteenth or early fourteenth century, with fourteenth-century additions

date_statements.label: target hand
date_statements.value: Hand A, late thirteenth or early fourteenth century
date_statements.notes: The normalized endpoint is a cataloging approximation of the descriptive phrase, not a colophon date.

physical_description.extent: ii modern paper endleaves + i medieval parchment endleaf + 240 or 243 leaves; foliated 1-239; target poems on ff. 198ra-200vb
physical_description.dimensions: approximately 280 x 170-180 mm; written space approximately 230 x 140 mm
notes: Most of ff. 198r-201v is written in two columns; the relevant portion belongs to the 45-line layout used through f. 200v.
Both target poems are in Hand A. The catalog localizes that hand's scribal dialect to West Oxfordshire; this is not proof of either poem's composition place.
The catalog describes the codex as English, possibly Oxford, but marks Oxford as uncertain.
The Sayings occupies ff. 198ra-199rb and lacks the first stanza found in other versions; the catalog explicitly corrects an older description of it as beginning imperfectly.
The Vision occupies ff. 199rb-200vb and is the sole witness of DIMEV 4814.
The two poems precede the Dispute between the Body and the Soul and form part of the eschatological cluster studied by Brent.

entities.label: Digital Bodleian complete color facsimile of MS. Laud Misc. 108
Bodleian Medieval Manuscripts catalog record for MS. Laud Misc. 108
DIMEV records 5215 and 4814, with related record 4564
notes: Record 5215 / NIMEV 3310 identifies the Laud Sayings and other witnesses; related record 4564 / NIMEV 2865 is the substantially corresponding Vernon version without two introductory stanzas.
Record 4814 / NIMEV 3089 identifies the 252-line Laud Vision as a unique witness version.

notes: Horstmann cites this preface only for Frederic Madden's descriptive English titles of the two Laud poems, not as a textual source.

J. Justin Brent, The Eschatological Cluster in Laud Misc. 108 (2011)

Justin Brent, ‘The Eschatological Cluster—Sayings of St. Bernard, Vision of St. Paul, and Dispute Between the Body and the Soul—in Oxford, Bodleian Library, MS Laud Misc. 108,’ in Kimberly K. Bell and Julie Nelson Couch, eds., The Texts and Contexts of Oxford, Bodleian Library, MS Laud Misc. 108 (Leiden: Brill, 2011), 157-176, https://doi.org/10.1163/9789004192249_010.

bibliographic.container_title: The Texts and Contexts of Oxford, Bodleian Library, MS Laud Misc. 108

entities.label: The Sayings of St. Bernard (Middle English work tradition)
entities.description: The abstract Middle English Sayings work represented by DIMEV 5215. Its five listed witnesses preserve different extents and openings; this entity does not collapse those copies into one physical object or settle their stemma.
notes: DIMEV 4564 describes a substantially corresponding Vernon version without two introductory stanzas; its exact work-level and stemmatic boundary remains unresolved.

entities.label: The Vision of St. Paul (Laud six-line Middle English version)
entities.description: The abstract 252-line, six-line-stanza Middle English Vision version uniquely witnessed in Laud Misc. 108. It remains distinct from the physical manuscript, Horstmann's edition, and the wider Visio Pauli tradition.

entities.label: Meditationes piissimae de cognitione humanae conditionis (Pseudo-Bernardian source tradition)
entities.description: Abstract Latin mortality and devotional source tradition to which Brent relates the Middle English Sayings. No physical Latin exemplar used by the Middle English adapter has been identified.

entities.label: Visio Pauli, Redaction IV
alternate_labels: Apocalypse of Paul, Latin Redaction IV
entities.description: Abstract Latin source version from which scholarship derives the Laud Middle English Vision. Brandes's 1885 printed text is a supporting edition of the redaction, not the unidentified physical exemplar used by the medieval adapter.

entities.label: Bodleian Library MS. Add. E. 6 (R)
entities.description: A parchment roll whose first poem is DIMEV 5215. DIMEV records a Latin invocation at both the beginning and end, but the current short catalog and Monda's edition do not assign folio or membrane locators.

date_statements.label: production
date_statements.value: second half of the thirteenth century

physical_description.extent: Approximately 6 feet long; also contains a 212-line Fifteen Signs poem and a 127-line Pater Noster exposition
notes: The official summary catalog describes religious Middle English poems on death, signs before the Last Judgment, and the Lord's Prayer; DIMEV identifies the Sayings as the first poem.
The dimensions and following-poem line counts are Monda's 1970 scholarly description, not measurements newly verified from a manuscript image.

entities.label: Bodleian Medieval Manuscripts catalog record for MS. Add. E. 6 (R)
Joseph B. Monda, The Sayings of Saint Bernard from MS Bodleian Additional E 6 (1970)
entities.description: Monda's article introduces and prints the previously unpublished Add. E. 6 roll witness. Its edited Middle English text occupies printed pp. 302-307; the preceding discussion describes the roll, dialect, date, provenance evidence, and relationship to Digby 86 and the other known versions.

bibliographic.citation: Joseph B. Monda, ‘The Sayings of Saint Bernard from MS Bodleian Additional E 6,’ Mediaeval Studies 32 (1970), 299-307, https://doi.org/10.1484/J.MS.2.306085.
bibliographic.title: The Sayings of Saint Bernard from MS Bodleian Additional E 6

entities.reproduction_format: Nine-page scholarly article with introduction, textual notes, and a six-page conservative manuscript edition
notes: Monda expands abbreviations except the ampersand, supplies missing letters in italics, places conjectural readings for illegible text in italic brackets after comparison with other manuscripts, ignores manuscript punctuation he considers accidental, and follows the manuscript's capitalization.

Monda argues that Add. E. 6 is structurally closer to the prototype than Digby 86 because it omits a stanza he considers spurious, while also judging the Add. E. 6 copy metrically rough and textually corrupt. This is an attributed scholarly argument, not a settled stemma.

entities.label: Internet Archive complete scan of Mediaeval Studies volume 32 (1970)
entities.description: A complete 364-page image-only PDF of the 1970 journal volume. Monda's article occupies PDF pages 307-315, corresponding to printed pp. 299-307.

entities.label: Bodleian Library MS. Digby 86
entities.description: Complete DIMEV 5215 witness on ff. 125v-127r, introduced by the Anglo-Norman rubric Les diz de seint bernard comencent ici tres beaus.

notes: The official record describes a trilingual miscellany produced in Worcestershire. That witness origin does not establish the poem's place of composition.

entities.reproduction_format: Complete 430-canvas full-color IIIF sequence cached at 1600 pixels wide

entities.description: DIMEV 5215 witness on ff. 106ra-107rb, lacking the first stanza present in Add. E. 6 and Digby 86 and ending before the full Laud sequence.

date_statements.label: British Library date for Part II

notes: The target belongs to the manuscript's second part and is reproduced textually as METS article 74.

entities.label: British Library Archives and Manuscripts catalog record for Harley MS 2253
The Complete Harley 2253 Manuscript, volumes 1-3
entities.description: Susanna Fein's complete-manuscript scholarly edition, with Anglo-Norman translations by David Raybin and Latin translations by Jan Ziolkowski. Article 74 presents The Sayings of Saint Bernard with a facing modern-English translation.

bibliographic.citation: The Complete Harley 2253 Manuscript, ed. Susanna Fein, with David Raybin and Jan Ziolkowski, 3 vols., Middle English Texts Series (Kalamazoo: Medieval Institute Publications, 2014-2015).
bibliographic.title: The Complete Harley 2253 Manuscript

entities.reproduction_format: Complete three-volume scholarly edition and translations, 1,448-page downloadable PDF plus web text
notes: The target occupies combined PDF pages 1112-1121, corresponding to article 74, printed pp. 84-93.

entities.label: Facsimile of British Museum MS. Harley 2253 (Ker, 1965)
entities.description: Neil Ripley Ker's EETS partial monochrome photographic facsimile reproduces Harley MS 2253 fols. 49-140 and 142, including the Sayings on ff. 106ra-107rb. It omits Harley fols. 1-48 and 141 and also includes fols. 68v and 76v of British Library Royal MS 12 C XII.

bibliographic.citation: Facsimile of British Museum MS. Harley 2253, with an introduction by N. R. Ker, Early English Text Society, Original Series 255 (London, New York, and Toronto: Oxford University Press, 1965).
bibliographic.title: Facsimile of British Museum MS. Harley 2253
bibliographic.series: Early English Text Society, Original Series 255
bibliographic.publisher: Oxford University Press for the Early English Text Society

entities.reproduction_format: Partial monochrome printed photographic facsimile with scholarly introduction; complete coverage of the target folios
notes: No complete public downloadable copy of this 1965 volume was verified during the dated review, so no local cache is recorded.

Google Books exposes bibliographic and snippet metadata and explicitly reports no ebook; the British Library catalogs an onsite/requestable copy as MS Facsimile 764; Facsimile Finder offers a current price-on-request purchase route.

entities.label: National Library of Scotland, Advocates MS 19.2.1 (Auchinleck Manuscript)
entities.description: Fragmentary DIMEV 5215 witness preserving only the final seven ubi sunt stanzas on f. 280ra.

notes: The manuscript was produced in London. That copying place is not treated as the Sayings' place of composition.

entities.label: NLS Auchinleck Manuscript digital facsimile project
entities.description: Provider-published image project for the principal Auchinleck codex and ten known detached leaves. The cached inventory preserves all 694 published surfaces; project completeness is not a claim that every image belongs to one currently bound object.
entities.reproduction_format: 694 provider-published IIIF image surfaces cached at 1600 pixels wide

notes: pdfinfo reports 365 pages; pdftotext exited 0 and extracted 902,623 bytes, including the target chapter.

qpdf --check exited 3 but reported that the operation succeeded with warnings. The warnings concern linearization hint-table offsets, shared-object offsets/counts, and a name/number-tree key-order repair attempted during validation.
The downloaded bytes are retained unchanged. No repaired or rewritten PDF was generated.

rights.component: Repository XML artifact
rights.basis: The pinned CME repository root LICENSE uses Unlicense wording but explicitly refers to software; its scope over the encoded medieval text, Horstmann transcription, and metadata is not established clearly enough to broaden.
notes: This statement does not cover Digital Bodleian photographs, Brill scholarship, DIMEV data, or modern catalog prose.

rights.component: U-M digital text distribution
rights.basis: The Corpus of Middle English Prose and Verse collection applies the RightsStatements.org No Copyright–United States statement while retaining provider terms for access and reuse.

rights.component: 1874 article text, transcription, and editorial matter
rights.basis: The article was published in 1874 and is in the public domain in the United States.

rights.component: Internet Archive scan files
rights.basis: Internet Archive metadata records possible-copyright-status NOT_IN_COPYRIGHT and copyright-region US; no separate scan-file license URL is supplied.

rights.component: Medieval linguistic and literary content of The Sayings of St. Bernard
rights.basis: The Middle English work is medieval and far predates U.S. copyright terms; modern photographs, catalog descriptions, editions, translations, and supplied reproductions remain distinct rights layers.

rights.component: Medieval linguistic and literary content of the Laud Vision of St. Paul
rights.basis: The Middle English work is medieval and far predates U.S. copyright terms; modern photographs, catalog descriptions, editions, translations, and supplied reproductions remain distinct rights layers.

rights.component: Digital Bodleian manuscript photographs and complete cached image bundle
rights.basis: The IIIF manifest attributes the photographs to Bodleian Libraries and states CC BY-NC 4.0. Digital Bodleian terms permit sharing and adaptation with attribution for noncommercial purposes; commercial reuse requires permission.
notes: Digital Bodleian's terms describe academic books and e-books up to 3,000 copies as noncommercial use, but uses intended for commercial advantage or monetary compensation require permission from imaging@bodleian.ox.ac.uk.
The local source-cache directory is Git-ignored; redistributing the bundle in a public or profit-oriented repository should be evaluated against the license and provider terms.

rights.component: Modern Bodleian catalog description and TEI encoding
rights.basis: The inspected pinned TEI file does not contain an item- or release-level license statement. No repository-wide license is imported as if it were an exact license for this catalog description.
notes: This correction removes an unsupported CC BY-NC-SA 3.0 claim; it does not affect the exact Digital Bodleian image manifests' separate CC BY-NC 4.0 statements.

rights.component: DIMEV v1.0 data release
rights.basis: The versioned DIMEV release identifies Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International.

rights.component: Internet Archive scan files for the 1868 Skeat volume
rights.basis: Internet Archive metadata records NOT_IN_COPYRIGHT in the United States; the underlying 1868 publication is public domain.

rights.component: Brent chapter and open-access book PDF, excluding separately credited third-party material
rights.basis: The Brill open-access book and chapter state Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial 4.0; third-party material may carry separate terms.

rights.component: Monda's 1970 article, introduction, scholarly edition, notes, and publisher PDF
rights.basis: The article was published in 1970, the current Brepols page marks the content as copyrighted, and no open reuse license was found on the publisher page or in the cached journal-volume scan.
notes: The public Internet Archive scan and the paid publisher route are access channels, not licenses to republish the modern article or edition text.

rights.component: Internet Archive delivery and scan layer for the complete 1970 journal volume
rights.basis: The Internet Archive item's metadata contains no licenseurl or rights field for the item or the volume-32 PDF, and its uploader is not identified as the journal publisher. Public download availability is not converted into an open-license claim.
notes: Rights in Monda's modern article are assessed separately and remain copyrighted.

rights.component: Medieval Latin source-work tradition
rights.basis: The Pseudo-Bernardian source tradition is medieval and predates modern copyright terms; later editions and reproductions remain separate.

rights.component: Medieval Latin Visio Pauli Redaction IV source-work tradition
rights.basis: The Latin source tradition is medieval and predates modern copyright terms; Brandes's edition and delivered scan are assessed separately.

rights.component: Modern Bodleian catalog description and TEI encoding
rights.basis: The inspected pinned TEI file does not contain an item- or release-level license statement; no broader repository assumption is converted into an exact catalog-data license.

rights.component: Modern Bodleian catalog description and TEI encoding
rights.basis: The inspected pinned TEI file does not contain an item- or release-level license statement; no broader repository assumption is converted into an exact catalog-data license.

rights.component: Complete Digital Bodleian manuscript image sequence
rights.basis: The exact IIIF manifest attributes the photographs to Bodleian Libraries and applies CC BY-NC 4.0.
notes: The complete private cache does not authorize redistribution or commercial print-on-demand use beyond the license and provider terms.

rights.component: Any newly supplied photographs or scans
rights.jurisdiction: Provider supply terms and intended place of reuse
rights.basis: No exact image object or order-specific license exists in the public record. The underlying medieval text and physical roll do not determine rights in newly supplied photography.

rights.component: British Library Archives and Manuscripts catalog text and metadata for Harley MS 2253
rights.basis: The current British Library catalog footer states that all catalog text is copyright British Library Board and available under CC BY 4.0 except where otherwise stated.
notes: This license is scoped to the catalog text and metadata. It is not a license for Harley MS 2253 manuscript photographs, legacy digitized images, newly supplied reproductions, or Ker's printed facsimile.

rights.component: British Library manuscript photography and any newly supplied reproduction
rights.jurisdiction: United Kingdom and provider supply terms
rights.basis: The catalog's metadata terms are not treated as an image license, no auditable current target-image object was available, and order-specific reuse terms would govern a supplied reproduction.

rights.component: 1965 photographic plates, publication layout, and Neil R. Ker's scholarly introduction
rights.jurisdiction: United Kingdom, United States, and applicable jurisdictions
rights.basis: The facsimile was published in 1965 and no open reuse license was found in the inspected British Library, Google Books, or vendor records. Medieval text and manuscript age do not determine rights in modern photography, layout, or Ker's introduction, and buying a physical copy does not itself grant reproduction rights.
notes: The British Library catalog footer's CC BY 4.0 statement applies to catalog text, not to this facsimile's photographic plates.

rights.component: METS scholarly text, translation, introductions, notes, and downloadable PDF
rights.basis: The scholarly edition and translations were published in 2014-2015. METS supplies open public access, but no explicit reuse license was found on the inspected article page or cached PDF.
notes: Open access is recorded as access and is not converted into a Creative Commons or public-domain claim.

rights.component: NLS project images, transcriptions, essays, descriptions, and reference material
rights.basis: The NLS project's citation and use page says all site materials are copyrighted, permits personal use by students, scholars, and the public, and strictly prohibits unauthorized commercial use or publication.
notes: Private Git-ignored research caching is kept separate from redistribution; publishing the bundle or project transcription, including in a commercial or print-on-demand workflow, requires written authorization.

rights.component: 1885 edition text and layout
rights.basis: The volume was published in 1885 and is in the public domain in the United States; the delivered scan package remains a separate layer.

rights.component: Internet Archive/Google scan delivery layer
rights.basis: No open license for the scan package was verified. The PDF carries Google Book Search usage guidance requesting personal, noncommercial use and retention of attribution; the historical 1885 edition is assessed separately.

editorial_practices.source_feature: Two adjacent poems within a much larger manuscript
editorial_practices.representation: A two-part six-page article headed I and II
editorial_practices.description: Horstmann selects the Sayings and Vision from ff. 198ra-200vb and prints each as a distinct numbered part; he does not reproduce the complete codex or the adjacent South English Legendary and Dispute texts.
editorial_practices.scope: Whole article; Sayings pp. 33-35 and Vision pp. 35-38

editorial_practices.source_feature: Individual Laud readings judged erroneous, ambiguous, or corrected above the line
editorial_practices.representation: German footnotes report the manuscript reading and Horstmann's proposed correction
editorial_practices.description: Horstmann prints a small apparatus of manuscript readings and conjectural corrections, including bisse/blisse in the Sayings and sewe with superscript iȝe in the Vision. These notes support direct manuscript consultation but do not imply collation with another textual witness.
notes: The schema term collation is used for the edition's explicit comparison of printed readings with the manuscript; Horstmann does not claim a multi-witness critical edition.

evidence.citation: General Corpus source XML CME00150.
evidence.summary: Confirms the CME identifier family, German article title and byline, two independent poem divisions, encoded text and notes, page markers, folio milestones, and repository fixity.

evidence.citation: Structural and textual audit of CME00150.xml on 2026-07-14.
evidence.locator: BODY/DIV1 and DIV2[1-2]
evidence.summary: Counted one DIV1, two DIV2 poem parts, 73 LG stanzas, 438 L verse lines, six PB elements, six manuscript-folio milestones, and ten notes. The Sayings has 31 stanzas / 186 lines and the Vision 42 stanzas / 252 lines.

evidence.citation: University of Michigan Library, Corpus of Middle English Prose and Verse.
evidence.summary: Confirms the public CME collection, its manual keying from public-domain editions, basic TEI delivery, and No Copyright–United States rights statement. The item route is retained despite the automated-access challenge encountered during review.

evidence.citation: Carl Horstmann, ‘Die Sprüche des h. Bernhard und die Vision des h. Paulus nach Ms. Laud 108,’ Archiv 52 (1874), 33-38; complete Internet Archive volume archivfrdasstu52brauuoft.
evidence.locator: printed pp. 33-38; PDF pages 45-50
evidence.summary: Primary evidence for the immediate source edition, publication details, two-part article boundary, direct Laud attribution, exact page division, text, notes, and citation of Skeat's preface.

evidence.citation: Page-by-page comparison of CME00150.xml with Horstmann 1874 on 2026-07-14.
evidence.locator: PDF pages 45-50 / printed pp. 33-38
evidence.summary: Verified complete article coverage and exact part boundaries; mapped printed pp. 33-38 to PDF pages 45-50 and the encoded folio milestones to Laud ff. 198b-200b.

evidence.citation: Internet Archive metadata for archivfrdasstu52brauuoft.
evidence.summary: Supplies the stable identifier, file inventory, scan provenance, NOT_IN_COPYRIGHT / US status, and stated-date evidence. Its inherited 1846 date describes the serial's beginning, not this 1874 volume.

evidence.citation: Bodleian Libraries, Medieval Manuscripts in Oxford Libraries, MS. Laud Misc. 108, manuscript_6917.
evidence.locator: items 2-3, ff. 198ra-200vb; physical description, hand description, and history
evidence.summary: Official evidence for shelfmark, exact loci, DIMEV IDs, dates, English and possible Oxford origin, parchment extent and dimensions, two-column target layout, Hand A, West Oxfordshire scribal dialect, adjacent contents, and Horstmann's edition of the Vision.

evidence.citation: Bodleian medieval-mss TEI source, MS_Laud_Misc_108.xml at commit cd36dce9a104617f960dab2c97ea78f95f917496.
evidence.summary: Pinned machine-readable source for the catalog facts, including exact item boundaries and the correction that the Sayings lacks another version's first stanza rather than beginning accidentally imperfect. The file contains no exact reuse license, so none is inferred.

evidence.citation: Digital Bodleian, MS. Laud Misc. 108, complete IIIF object c5438c30-71dd-4cec-ac59-eb1e5a11f2a4.
evidence.locator: 496 canvases; target canvases 411-416
evidence.summary: Complete color facsimile and authoritative canvas sequence. The manifest supplies Bodleian attribution and CC BY-NC 4.0 terms; the local bundle retains all canvases, manifest, and exact-source inventory.

evidence.citation: Visual inspection of the complete Laud Misc. 108 IIIF bundle and target canvases on 2026-07-14.
evidence.summary: Verified a complete 496-image bundle, clean ZIP integrity, exact folio labels and UUIDs, the Sayings on canvases 411-413, the Vision on canvases 413-416, and the shared poem and adjacent-work boundary surfaces.

evidence.citation: Digital Bodleian, Terms of Use.
evidence.summary: Defines attribution and noncommercial reuse of Digital Bodleian images, treats qualifying academic publications up to 3,000 copies as noncommercial, and directs commercial-use permission requests to imaging@bodleian.ox.ac.uk.

evidence.citation: Digital Index of Middle English Verse, records 5215, 4814, and related record 4564.
evidence.locator: records 5215 / NIMEV 3310; 4814 / NIMEV 3089; 4564 / NIMEV 2865
evidence.summary: Supports separate work identities, incipits, six-line aabccb form, the five DIMEV 5215 witnesses and their exact extents (Add. E. 6 roll; Digby 86 ff. 125v-127r; Laud 108 ff. 198ra-199rb; Harley 2253 ff. 106ra-107rb; Auchinleck f. 280ra final seven stanzas), the related DIMEV 4564 version, the 252-line Vision extent, and the Vision's unique-witness status. Record 5215's Bernard authorship field is treated as traditional attribution, not verified authorship.

evidence.citation: Digital Index of Middle English Verse v1.0 source release.
evidence.summary: Versioned complete data source for the three consulted records and their CC BY 4.0 license; the cached ZIP passed integrity testing.

evidence.citation: Walter W. Skeat, ed., The Lay of Havelok the Dane (1868), Preface p. xxxv; Internet Archive item layofhavelokdane00madduoft.
evidence.locator: printed Preface p. xxxv; PDF page 47
evidence.summary: Confirms Skeat's quotation of Madden's Laud contents description naming ‘the Sayings of St Bernard and the Visions of St Paul, both in six-line stanzas,’ the exact passage cited by Horstmann's title footnote.

evidence.citation: Internet Archive metadata for layofhavelokdane00madduoft.
evidence.summary: Supplies the stable identifier, file inventory, scan provenance, and NOT_IN_COPYRIGHT / US status for the complete supporting volume.

evidence.citation: J. Justin Brent, ‘The Eschatological Cluster,’ in The Texts and Contexts of Oxford, Bodleian Library, MS Laud Misc. 108 (Brill, 2011), 157-176.
evidence.locator: printed pp. 157-176; complete-book PDF pages 180-199
evidence.summary: Modern scholarship supporting the three-poem cluster context, common Hand A and stanza form of the first two poems, the Sayings' loose adaptation of Pseudo-Bernardian Meditationes, the Vision's descent from the fourth Latin Visio Pauli redaction, themes, witness relationships, and the unresolved status of immediate exemplars and compositional intervention. pdfinfo reports 365 pages and pdftotext successfully extracts the complete file. qpdf exits 3 with recoverable linearization/shared-object/name-tree warnings; the original cached bytes were retained without repair or rewrite.

evidence.citation: Brill open-access license statement for The Texts and Contexts of Oxford, Bodleian Library, MS Laud Misc. 108 and Brent's chapter.
evidence.summary: States CC BY-NC 4.0 for the open-access book/chapter while warning that separately credited third-party material may require separate permission.

evidence.citation: Kyvero Press CME repository root LICENSE at pinned commit e1d77c88ca561be924b2fedc54dd474be2e7e49c.
evidence.locator: Unlicense wording scoped to software
evidence.summary: Documents the repository license and its explicit software wording; it does not establish rights in inherited textual data, Horstmann's edition, manuscript images, catalog data, or supporting scholarship.

evidence.citation: Bodleian Libraries, Medieval Manuscripts in Oxford Libraries, MS. Add. E. 6 (R), manuscript_184; pinned catalog TEI at commit cd36dce9a104617f960dab2c97ea78f95f917496.
evidence.locator: MS_Add_E_6_R; item 1; objectDesc form=roll; origDate 1250-1300
evidence.summary: Official evidence for the exact shelfmark, parchment-roll format, English origin, second-half-thirteenth-century date, broad religious-poem contents, ARKs, Special Collections contact, and the absence of an item-level license statement in the pinned TEI. DIMEV, not this short record, identifies the Sayings as the first poem.

evidence.citation: Joseph B. Monda, ‘The Sayings of Saint Bernard from MS Bodleian Additional E 6,’ Mediaeval Studies 32 (1970), 299-307, https://doi.org/10.1484/J.MS.2.306085.
evidence.locator: complete-volume PDF pages 307-315 / printed pp. 299-307, a constant +8 PDF-page offset; edited Add. E. 6 text on PDF pages 310-315 / printed pp. 302-307
evidence.summary: Primary scholarly evidence for Monda's direct edition of the first poem on Add. E. 6, the article's exact page boundary, editorial method, the roll's approximately 6 ft by 3 3/8 in dimensions and three-poem contents, second-half-thirteenth-century date, Southeast Midland/possible Essex witness evidence, and Monda's qualified comparison of Add. E. 6 with Digby 86 and the other versions. The complete 364-page image-only volume was page-mapped by rendering and OCR inspection; qpdf exits 3 with two nonfatal linearization/outline-offset warnings, and the cached bytes were not repaired or rewritten.

evidence.citation: Internet Archive metadata for MediaevalStudies, including Mediaeval Studies - Volume 32 - 1970.pdf.
evidence.locator: item identifier MediaevalStudies; file Mediaeval Studies - Volume 32 - 1970.pdf
evidence.summary: Confirms the exact complete-volume filename and 21,092,296-byte size, with Internet Archive MD5 6c9e8915492350ca14aae1f333d280eb and SHA-1 8f7e4aa41922d7d59361b496a1f87bee9d14cced. The item metadata names the Pontifical Institute of Mediaeval Studies as creator but contains no licenseurl or rights field and identifies a non-institutional uploader, so public access is not treated as an open license.

evidence.citation: Bodleian Libraries, Medieval Manuscripts in Oxford Libraries, MS. Digby 86, manuscript_4426; pinned catalog TEI at commit cd36dce9a104617f960dab2c97ea78f95f917496.
evidence.locator: item 41, ff. 125v-127r; origDate c. 1272-1282
evidence.summary: Official evidence for the exact shelfmark and Sayings locus, title, Anglo-Norman rubric, incipit, c. 1272-1282 Worcestershire production, parchment-codex context, and the absence of an item-level license statement in the pinned TEI.

evidence.citation: Digital Bodleian, MS. Digby 86, complete IIIF object 2d6dfc9f-2ab9-46bf-9b32-e52f0e6eabb3.
evidence.locator: 430 canvases; Sayings ff. 125v-127r / canvases 258-261 / bundle images 000258-000261
evidence.summary: Complete provider-published color facsimile, authoritative canvas sequence, exact work mapping, and item-level CC BY-NC 4.0 attribution. The hard-linked local bundle retains the provider manifest, exact-source inventory, and all 430 images.

evidence.citation: Bodleian Libraries, Special Collections remote copying and Imaging Services.
evidence.summary: Current official routes for requesting reference scans or commissioned photography and for determining reuse terms for supplied images.

evidence.citation: British Library Archives and Manuscripts Catalogue, Harley MS 2253, record 040-002048084.
evidence.locator: identity, Part II date and origin, material, access, Digitised Content fields, and site footer
evidence.summary: Confirms current shelfmark, parchment-codex context, circa 1330-1340 date for Part II, and the current contradiction between a full-digital-coverage statement and an unavailable legacy image service. DIMEV and METS supply the exact ff. 106ra-107rb work mapping. The current site footer licenses British Library catalog text under CC BY 4.0 except where otherwise stated; that catalog-text license is not applied to manuscript images.

evidence.citation: British Library Archives and Manuscripts Catalogue, MS Facsimile 764, record 040-003312095.
evidence.locator: reference MS Facsimile 764; scope and content; 1965 date; access conditions; site footer
evidence.summary: Official evidence that the British Library holds a 1965 EETS facsimile of Harley MS 2253 with an introduction by N. R. Ker under shelfmark MS Facsimile 764 and provides onsite/request access. The record does not expose a digital copy. Its footer's CC BY 4.0 statement applies to catalog text, not the facsimile plates.

evidence.citation: Google Books metadata and snippet view for Facsimile of British Museum Ms. Harley 2253, volume ID 3OTXbXbT0n8C.
evidence.locator: bibliographic information, contents snippets, and access statement
evidence.summary: Supports the title, 1965 publication, EETS issue 255, Neil Ripley Ker contribution, 215-page length, and a contents entry for Harley MS 2253 fols. 49-140 and 142. Google Books reports snippet view and no ebook available, so this is metadata evidence rather than a complete digital facsimile.

evidence.citation: Facsimile Finder, Harley Lyrics: Facsimile of British Museum MS. Harley 2253 facsimile edition.
evidence.locator: facsimile edition description and Request Info / Price route
evidence.summary: Current vendor evidence for an Oxford University Press 1965 partial monochrome facsimile covering Harley fols. 49-140 and 142 and adding Royal MS 12 C XII fols. 68v and 76v. The page offers a Request Info / Price quote route but displays no numerical price; it is used for current purchase availability and physical coverage, not as authority for textual lineage or reuse rights.

evidence.citation: Susanna Fein, ed., with David Raybin and Jan Ziolkowski, The Complete Harley 2253 Manuscript, article 74, The Sayings of Saint Bernard (Middle English Texts Series, 2014-2015).
evidence.locator: article 74; printed pp. 84-93; combined PDF pp. 1112-1121; Harley ff. 106ra-107rb
evidence.summary: Current complete scholarly transcription and facing translation of the Harley Sayings witness. The 1,448-page complete-edition PDF is cached; target pages render and extract despite disclosed nonfatal qpdf linearization warnings.

evidence.citation: National Library of Scotland, Auchinleck Manuscript, The Sayings of St Bernard and complete published facsimile project.
evidence.locator: final seven stanzas on f. 280ra; image 567 of 694, label 280r; images/000567.jpg
evidence.summary: Official evidence for the fragmentary final-seven-stanza witness, the 1331-1340 London manuscript context, and the complete provider-published image inventory. The bundle preserves all 694 project surfaces, including detached leaves held outside the principal codex, and maps only column a of f. 280r to the target.

evidence.citation: National Library of Scotland, Auchinleck Manuscript, How to refer to this site.
evidence.summary: States that site texts, essays, images, descriptions, and reference materials are copyrighted, permits personal use by students, scholars, and the public, and prohibits unauthorized commercial use or publication.

evidence.citation: Herman Brandes, Visio S. Pauli: Ein Beitrag zur Visionslitteratur, mit einem deutschen und zwei lateinischen Texten (Halle: Max Niemeyer, 1885).
evidence.locator: complete volume; Latin Redaction IV printed pp. 73-80, PDF pp. 87-94, viewer leaves n86-n93
evidence.summary: Confirms Brandes's complete 1885 publication and supplies a supporting printed text of Latin Redaction IV. It is not identified as the physical exemplar used by the medieval Middle English adapter.

open_questions.question: How are all five DIMEV 5215 Sayings witnesses and DIMEV 4564's Vernon version stemmatically related, and did Laud Hand A compose or add the four Laud-specific stanzas?
next_steps: Collate Laud 108 against Bodleian MS Add. E.6, Digby 86, Harley 2253, the Auchinleck fragment, and the Vernon-version witnesses.
Distinguish common archetypal wording, local revision, accidental loss, and Hand A's possible intervention.
Retain the joint uncertain 1250-1282 first-attestation window from Add. E. 6 (1250-1300) and Digby 86 (c. 1272-1282) without selecting a false singular earliest witness.

open_questions.question: Which Latin or vernacular exemplar and exact fourth-redaction branch underlie the unique Laud six-line Vision, and was this version composed or revised specifically to follow the Sayings?
next_steps: Collate the Laud Vision against critical editions and manuscripts of the fourth redaction of the Latin Visio Pauli.
Separate inherited Apocalypse of Paul material, Sunday Respite material, translation choices, abbreviation, and possible cluster-specific composition.
Treat Brent's cluster-composition proposal as a scholarly possibility until textual comparison strengthens it.

open_questions.question: What item-level production date, keying documentation, proof sheets, and transformation history connect Horstmann's print article to U-M item CME00150?
next_steps: Request item-level production and proofing documentation from the University of Michigan Library.
Compare the current HTML delivery and any bulk-source file against the pinned repository XML.
Keep the immediate edition relationship high-confidence while leaving undocumented keying intermediaries unspecified.

open_questions.question: Can either poem's place of composition be established independently of Laud Hand A's West Oxfordshire scribal dialect and the manuscript's tentative Oxford production history?
next_steps: Compare language across all Sayings witnesses before assigning any composition region.
Study the Vision's unique language against Hand A's other work and relevant source traditions.
Continue to scope West Oxfordshire to the witness scribe rather than the abstract poems.

open_questions.question: Can the Bodleian supply or identify a complete digital reproduction of the unfoliated MS. Add. E. 6 (R) roll and map the Sayings to exact membranes or physical segments?
next_steps: Ask first whether an existing preservation or legacy surrogate can be delivered.

Request both sides of every membrane, seams, endorsements, guards, labels, storage features, edges where offered, and color targets; identify DIMEV 5215 as the first poem rather than requesting only a cropped text excerpt.
Obtain a membrane/segment concordance and exact written terms for private storage, scholarly publication, public Git redistribution, and commercial print-on-demand use.

open_questions.question: Can a lawful complete digital copy of Ker's 1965 Facsimile of British Museum MS. Harley 2253 be obtained, and under what private-research and republication terms?
next_steps: Ask the British Library whether MS Facsimile 764 can be digitized or supplied for private research and request exact reuse terms.
Ask the Early English Text Society or Oxford University Press whether a licensed digital edition exists.
If purchasing the quoted physical volume, inspect and record exact plate/page mappings for ff. 106ra-107rb without assuming that purchase authorizes digitization or redistribution.
